source.document: FIDIC Yellow Book 1999 vs Red Book 1999

differences.red_book: Employer provides design (drawings + specifications). Contractor builds to Employer's design.
differences.yellow_book: Contractor designs and builds to Employer's Requirements. Contractor responsible for design adequacy.
differences.engineers_implication: Engineer reviews Contractor's design documents but review does not transfer responsibility

differences.red_book: Drawings + Specifications provided by Employer. Schedule of rates/BoQ for payment.
differences.yellow_book: Employer's Requirements + Contractor's Proposal. Schedule of payments for lump sum pricing.
differences.engineers_implication: Document priority and ambiguity resolution differs -- Employer's Requirements replace Specifications

differences.red_book: No design review by Engineer (Employer's design). Engineer reviews Contractor's submissions for construction compliance only.
differences.yellow_book: Engineer reviews Contractor's design documents within 21 days (Sub-Clause 5.2). Four response options: approve, comment-no objection, comment-disapprove, reject.
differences.engineers_implication: Engineer must review within 21 days. Review does NOT relieve Contractor of responsibility. Critical distinction.

differences.red_book: Re-measurement: payment based on actual measured quantities at contract rates. Quantity risk with Employer.
differences.yellow_book: Lump sum: payment based on milestones or percentage completion. Quantity risk with Contractor.
differences.engineers_implication: Monthly certificates based on progress milestones, not measured quantities. Contractor bears quantity risk.

differences.red_book: Tests on Completion (Clause 9) -- primarily construction quality tests.
differences.yellow_book: Tests on Completion with performance criteria (Sub-Clause 9.4). Must demonstrate Works meet Employer's Requirements performance specifications.
differences.engineers_implication: Performance testing is more significant. Failure may indicate design defect (Contractor's risk) not just construction defect.

differences.yellow_book: Clause 12 -- detailed provisions for Tests after Completion to verify long-term performance. Contractor liable for performance deficiencies.
differences.engineers_implication: May need to monitor and certify performance over an extended period after Taking-Over.

differences.red_book: No fitness-for-purpose obligation (Contractor builds to Employer's design).
differences.yellow_book: Sub-Clause 5.3: Contractor undertakes that completed Works will be fit for intended purpose stated in Employer's Requirements.
differences.engineers_implication: Higher standard than reasonable skill and care. Contractor warrants fitness for purpose.

differences.red_book: Sub-Clause 1.5: Contract Agreement > Letter of Acceptance > Letter of Tender > Particular Conditions > General Conditions > Specification > Drawings > Schedules.
differences.yellow_book: Sub-Clause 1.5: Similar but Employer's Requirements and Contractor's Proposal replace Specification and Drawings in the hierarchy.
differences.engineers_implication: Ambiguity resolution follows different document hierarchy. Employer's Requirements take precedence over Contractor's Proposal.

differences.red_book: Variations to the Works as designed by the Employer. Engineer instructs changes to design.
differences.yellow_book: Variations to the Employer's Requirements or other contract provisions. Contractor designs the changed work.
differences.engineers_implication: Engineer varies the requirement, not the design. Contractor designs the solution to meet the varied requirement.

differences.red_book: Employer bears design risk (Employer provided the design). Design errors are Employer's liability.
differences.yellow_book: Contractor bears design risk (Contractor provided the design). Design errors are Contractor's liability.
differences.engineers_implication: When assessing claims, design-related issues are Contractor's risk under Yellow Book (opposite to Red Book).
